fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small editing engine for rich-text content — basically the brain behind a formatting toolbar. Users have been complaining that when they try to bold something or turn a paragraph into a quote, nothing sticks consistently, and the floating toolbar keeps popping up in the wrong spot relative to where they've highlighted text. Super annoying UX.

The engine needs to handle toggling character-level styles (think the usual suspects), switching block structures, and managing link attachment/detachment on selected ranges. For the link stuff, remember we had that URL normalization pattern from the auth flow we built — same idea applies here, just adapt it for this context.

The toolbar positioning math should follow whatever convention we settled on in the design spec (check the spacing constants we agreed on). Output needs to be line-based key=value pairs so our test harness can compare things directly.

One big thing: the code can't be one giant file. Split it up by responsibility. The part that reads stdin and spits out stdout should be totally separate from the actual logic. Unknown or broken inputs should fail gracefully — no stack traces leaking out.

Test runner lives under rcb_tests/ and should handle multiple case directories without stomping on previous results. Can someone take a pass at this and make sure the public cases all pass?

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the floating toolbar math, the top position should use a fixed gap of 14 units above the selection, so the formula is exactly offset_top = range.top - editor.top - (toolbar.height + 14). For the horizontal position, it should be centered over the selection, using offset_left = (range.left - editor.left) + (range.right - range.left) / 2 - toolbar.width / 2. Values may be fractional.

Also, on structure, this really does need to stay split cleanly by responsibility. We have four distinct core areas here: inline formatting, block formatting, hyperlink lifecycle, and toolbar geometry. Then the I/O adapter (stdin/stdout + JSON parsing) should stay as a separate thin layer from the core logic. Parsing, routing, validation, core transformation, and output formatting should each be separate concerns rather than blended together.

For link handling, if the entered address already begins with http:// or https://, it is stored unchanged. If it has no scheme, http:// is prepended before storing. For example, www.example.com becomes http://www.example.com. Same rule applies in Feature 3.1 (apply_hyperlink): prepend http:// if the address has no scheme, leave http:// or https:// addresses unchanged. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature3_1_apply_link.json and the add_link command handler. And when a link gets removed, the result should report both fields exactly as url_before=<the URL that existed before removal> and entity_after=none.

One other formatting detail: if multiple inline formats are active at the same time, list them comma-separated in sorted (alphabetical) order, for example BOLD,ITALIC,UNDERLINE.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the cursor is collapsed (no characters selected), toggling a format turns it into a pending style. The format is reported as active immediately after toggling it on, even though no text is selected.

trigger_keywords: case index zero padding in filename
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each test case writes one file to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt — for example, the first case (index 0) in feature1_inline_style.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases produces rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_inline_style@000.txt.

trigger_keywords: what directory does test.sh use by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test runner (rcb_tests/test.sh) defaults to the 'test_cases' subdirectory. It accepts --cases-dir <subdir> to override this. Running with different case directories never overwrites each other because output is namespaced by <cases-dir>.

trigger_keywords: how to report errors without stack traces
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Unknown commands and malformed input must be handled gracefully and reported as neutral error categories — never as raw runtime traces or exception messages. The adapter catches these and emits a neutral error line in key=value format.

trigger_keywords: what format are block type names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Inline format names are opaque uppercase identifiers, e.g. BOLD, ITALIC, UNDERLINE. Block type identifiers are opaque lowercase hyphenated strings, e.g. header-two, blockquote, unordered-list-item.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: whatever convention we settled on in the design spec (check the spacing constants we agreed on)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fixed 14-unit vertical gap used in the toolbar positioning formula: offset_top = range.top - editor.top - (toolbar.height + 14). Defined in Feature 4 of start.md and verified in feature4_toolbar_position.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have documented on my end — you'll want to dig into the test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the spec in start.md to confirm the exact behavior. Can you check there and let me know what you find?

feature1_inline_style.json.description: Toggling an inline character format on a text run reports which inline formats are now active. With an empty (collapsed) cursor the chosen format becomes a pending style that applies to text typed next.

feature2_block_type.json.description: Toggling a block-level format on the block containing the cursor converts that block to the requested block type, reported back as the resulting block type.

feature3_1_apply_link.json.description: Applying a hyperlink to a selected text range stores a link entity carrying the target URL. When the entered address has no scheme, a default http:// scheme is prepended; an address that already starts with http:// or https:// is stored unchanged.

feature3_2_remove_link.json.description: Removing a hyperlink from a previously linked, non-empty selection detaches the link entity from the text. The output reports the URL that existed before removal and that no entity remains attached afterward.

feature4_toolbar_position.json.description: Computes where a floating formatting toolbar should be placed so it is horizontally centered over the current text selection and sits a fixed gap above it. Inputs are the selection rectangle, the editor's bounding rectangle, and the toolbar size; outputs are the toolbar's top and left offsets relative to the editor.
